summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is a trophic level?

A trophic level describes an organism's position in a food chain based on what it eats. The first trophic level consists of producers (autotrophs), the second of primary consumers (herbivores), the third of secondary consumers (carnivores), and so on.

What is the key difference between a food chain and a food web?

A food chain is a simplified model showing a single linear sequence of energy transfer (e.g., grass → rabbit → fox). A food web is a more realistic and complex model showing all the interconnected food chains within an ecosystem, reflecting that most organisms eat and are eaten by multiple species.

What is primary productivity?

Primary productivity measures the rate at which autotrophs (producers) convert inorganic energy sources (sunlight or chemical energy) into organic compounds (biomass). It is the foundation of energy flow in all ecosystems because it determines how much energy enters the food web.

What is the relationship between gross primary productivity (GPP) and net primary productivity (NPP)?

GPP is the total rate of energy captured by producers through photosynthesis. Producers use some of this energy for their own cellular respiration. NPP = GPP − Respiration(producers). NPP represents the energy stored in plant biomass that is actually available to herbivores and the rest of the food web.

What is biological magnification (biomagnification)?

Biomagnification occurs when persistent toxins that cannot be broken down or excreted accumulate in organisms' tissues. At each trophic level, a consumer ingests many prey organisms (each containing some toxin), so the toxin concentration increases. Top predators (e.g., eagles, tuna, humans) can have toxin levels millions of times higher than concentrations in the water or bottom of the food chain.

At the cellular level, what is chemiosmosis and how does it relate to energy flow?

Chemiosmosis is the mechanism by which the proton (H⁺) gradient across a membrane (built by the electron transport chain) drives ATP synthesis through ATP synthase. This process occurs in both mitochondria (cellular respiration) and chloroplasts (photosynthesis), making it a universal mechanism of energy conversion in living systems.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

This classic example demonstrates biomagnification. DDT is lipophilic (fat-soluble), non-biodegradable, and has a long half-life. At each trophic level, organisms consume many prey items, each carrying DDT in their fat tissues. The toxin concentrates approximately 10× at each level (paralleling the 10% energy rule). In ospreys, DDT interfered with eggshell formation (inhibiting calcium deposition), causing population crashes — a key finding that led to DDT bans.
